name: Uganda
tagline: From the great lakeside kingdoms of the Buganda kabakas to a turbulent modern republic.
summary: Uganda lies at the heart of Africa's interlacustrine region, the well-watered country between the great lakes where Bantu farmers and Nilotic herders met and built some of the continent's most enduring kingdoms — above all Buganda, ruled by its kabaka, alongside Bunyoro-Kitara, Ankole and Toro. In the nineteenth century Swahili-Arab traders, then Christian missionaries, reached the court of Buganda, and religious rivalry led to the killing of the Uganda Martyrs in 1885–87. Britain declared the Uganda Protectorate in 1894 and through the 1900 Buganda Agreement governed by indirect rule, while the Uganda Railway, built largely by Indian labour, bound the country to the coast. Uganda became independent on 9 October 1962 with Milton Obote as prime minister and the kabaka Mutesa II as its first president. Independence gave way to upheaval: Obote's abolition of the kingdoms in 1966–67, Idi Amin's brutal dictatorship of 1971–79 and the expulsion of the Asian community, the war that ousted Amin, a second Obote period and the Bush War, and Yoweri Museveni's seizure of power in 1986. The north was then ravaged for two decades by the Lord's Resistance Army.

founding.label: Independence from the United Kingdom
founding.detail: Uganda became an independent state on 9 October 1962, ending sixty-eight years of British rule that had begun with the proclamation of the Uganda Protectorate in 1894. Independence was negotiated through constitutional conferences in London, which preserved a special federal status for the kingdom of Buganda and the other kingdoms. Milton Obote, leader of the Uganda People's Congress, became the country's first prime minister and head of government; a year later, in October 1963, Uganda became a republic and the kabaka of Buganda, Sir Edward Mutesa II, was elected its first (largely ceremonial) president. The date 9 October remains Uganda's Independence Day.

quickFacts.label: Independence
quickFacts.value: 9 October 1962 from the United Kingdom; republic October 1963

quickFacts.label: First leaders
quickFacts.value: Milton Obote (prime minister); Sir Edward Mutesa II, kabaka of Buganda (first president, 1963–66)

quickFacts.label: Colonial rule
quickFacts.value: Uganda Protectorate from 1894; Buganda Agreement of 1900 entrenched indirect rule

quickFacts.label: Idi Amin era
quickFacts.value: Military dictatorship 1971–79; some 80,000 Asians expelled in 1972; up to 300,000 killed

quickFacts.label: UNESCO site
quickFacts.value: Tombs of Buganda Kings at Kasubi, inscribed 2001; burial place of four kabakas

eras.title: Peoples and the Great Lakes Kingdoms
eras.standfirst: Bantu farmers and Nilotic herders settled the interlacustrine region and built the kingdoms of Bunyoro-Kitara, Buganda, Ankole and Toro.
body: The fertile, well-watered country between the great lakes of East Africa — the interlacustrine region — drew successive waves of settlers over more than two millennia. Iron-using Bantu-speaking farmers spread into the area in the last centuries BCE and the early centuries CE, clearing forest for banana gardens and cultivating the densely populated lands north and west of Lake Victoria. They were followed by Nilotic-speaking peoples, including Luo groups moving down from the upper Nile region, who brought cattle-keeping and new ruling dynasties. The mingling of these farming and pastoral traditions shaped the societies and kingdoms of what would become Uganda.

The earliest of the great states was Bunyoro-Kitara, ruled by the omukama, which by tradition succeeded the semi-legendary empire associated with the Bachwezi. From the collapse of Bunyoro's wider authority emerged a cluster of rival kingdoms. To the east lay Buganda, ruled by its kabaka and reckoned in tradition to have been founded by the figure Kintu (or, in Bunyoro tradition, by Kato Kimera of the Babito line). To the southwest, the kingdom of Ankole grew around a cattle-owning aristocracy of Bahima pastoralists ruling Bairu cultivators, headed by the omugabe. Around the 1830s, the kingdom of Toro broke away from Bunyoro under Prince Kaboyo.

These kingdoms competed for land, cattle and tribute across the centuries. Compact, well-organised Buganda, governed through a hierarchy of chiefs answerable to the kabaka rather than to hereditary clan heads, proved especially expansionist, seizing territory from Bunyoro under a succession of able kings and growing into the largest power in the region by the nineteenth century. The dead kings of Buganda were honoured at royal tombs, a tradition that would culminate in the great burial site at Kasubi.

events.title: Bantu farmers settle the lakes region
events.summary: Iron-using Bantu-speaking agriculturalists spread into the interlacustrine region north and west of Lake Victoria, establishing the banana-farming societies that underlie the later kingdoms.

events.title: Rise of Bunyoro-Kitara
events.summary: The kingdom of Bunyoro-Kitara, ruled by the omukama and linked in tradition to the Bachwezi, emerges as the dominant power of the interlacustrine region.

events.title: Foundation of Buganda
events.summary: Buganda takes shape east of Bunyoro under its first kabakas; tradition credits its founding to Kintu or, in Bunyoro accounts, to Kato Kimera of the Babito dynasty.

events.title: Buganda expands at Bunyoro's expense
events.summary: Under a line of aggressive kabakas, the compact kingdom of Buganda annexes territory from Bunyoro-Kitara, growing into the largest state in the interlacustrine region.

pullquote.text: Buganda was governed through a ladder of chiefs answerable to the kabaka, a system that made it the most powerful of the lakes kingdoms.

eras.title: Traders, Missionaries and the Uganda Martyrs
eras.standfirst: Swahili-Arab traders brought Islam and guns to the kabaka's court, Christian missionaries followed, and religious rivalry led to the killing of the Uganda Martyrs.
body: From the 1840s, Swahili-Arab caravans financed from Zanzibar pushed into the interlacustrine kingdoms in search of ivory and enslaved people, exchanging cloth, guns and gunpowder. Around 1844, a trader remembered as Ahmed bin Ibrahim reached the court of Kabaka Suna II of Buganda, the first such visitor recorded in the kingdom's traditions. His successor, Kabaka Mutesa I, welcomed the traders, took an interest in Islam and for a time observed Muslim practice, and used the firearms they brought to strengthen Buganda against its rivals.

Christian missionaries arrived in the wake of the explorers. After Henry Morton Stanley publicised Mutesa's apparent openness to Christianity, the Church Missionary Society (Anglican) sent agents to Buganda in 1877, followed by Roman Catholic missionaries of the White Fathers in 1879. The kabaka's court thus became host to three competing faiths — Muslim, Anglican (Protestant) and Catholic — alongside the older religion of the kingdom, and converts gathered among the royal pages and chiefs. Mutesa I balanced the rivals warily until his death in 1884.

His young successor, Kabaka Mwanga II, came to see the growing body of Christian converts at court as a threat to his authority. Between 1885 and 1887 he ordered a series of executions of Christian pages and officials who defied his commands. The most notorious mass killing came on 3 June 1886, when a group including Charles Lwanga, master of the royal pages, was burned to death at Namugongo. Twenty-two of the Catholic victims were later canonised, and the Uganda Martyrs — Catholic and Anglican alike — became central figures in the country's Christian memory.

events.title: Swahili-Arab traders reach Buganda
events.summary: A caravan led by Ahmed bin Ibrahim reaches the court of Kabaka Suna II, opening Buganda to the Zanzibar-based ivory and slave trade and to Islam.

events.title: Anglican missionaries arrive
events.summary: Agents of the Church Missionary Society reach Buganda after Stanley publicises Mutesa I's apparent interest in Christianity, beginning Protestant evangelism at the kabaka's court.

events.title: Catholic White Fathers arrive
events.summary: Roman Catholic missionaries of the White Fathers reach Buganda, adding a third faith to the rivalry of Muslim, Protestant and Catholic factions at court.

events.title: Martyrdom of Charles Lwanga
events.summary: Kabaka Mwanga II has Charles Lwanga and a group of Christian pages burned to death at Namugongo, the most notorious episode in the killing of the Uganda Martyrs of 1885–87.

events.title: Killing of the Uganda Martyrs
events.summary: Mwanga II executes some forty-five Catholic and Anglican converts who refuse to renounce their faith; twenty-two Catholic victims were later canonised as saints.

figures.name: Mutesa I
figures.blurb: The powerful kabaka who welcomed Swahili-Arab traders and the first Christian missionaries, balancing Muslim, Protestant and Catholic factions at his court while strengthening Buganda with imported guns.

figures.name: Charles Lwanga
figures.role: Master of the royal pages; Catholic martyr
figures.blurb: Chief of the royal pages at Mwanga II's court, burned to death at Namugongo on 3 June 1886 for his Christian faith; canonised as a saint and patron of Africa's youth.

pullquote.text: Between 1885 and 1887, on the kabaka's orders, Catholic and Anglican converts went to their deaths rather than renounce their new faith.

eras.title: The Uganda Protectorate
eras.standfirst: Britain ruled through the kabaka and chiefs under the Buganda Agreement of 1900, drove the Uganda Railway inland, and relied on Indian labour and traders.
body: After the Imperial British East Africa Company handed over its rights, Britain declared the Uganda Protectorate in 1894, initially over Buganda and then extending across the wider territory of present-day Uganda. Rather than govern directly, the British adopted indirect rule, working through the existing kingdoms and chiefs. The cornerstone of this system was the Buganda Agreement, signed at Mengo on 10 March 1900 between the imperial commissioner Sir Harry Johnston and the regents of the infant Kabaka Daudi Chwa II. It recognised the kabaka and the Lukiiko (the Buganda council), divided land between the Crown and some thousand chiefs and notables under the freehold 'mailo' system, and imposed hut and gun taxes, giving Buganda a privileged, semi-autonomous status within the protectorate.

To open the interior, the colonial authorities built the Uganda Railway from the coast at Mombasa, begun in 1896 and reaching Kisumu on Lake Victoria in 1901. Though it ran mainly through Kenya, the line was built to serve Uganda and was constructed by a workforce of some 32,000 indentured labourers brought from British India, thousands of whom died of disease, accidents and, near Tsavo, lion attacks. Many Indian workers, traders and clerks settled in East Africa, and South Asians came to play a central role in Uganda's commerce, an economic position that would later make them a target.

Under indirect rule, Buganda's Protestant chiefs, led by the powerful katikkiro (chief minister) Apolo Kagwa, prospered and helped extend British authority over the rest of the protectorate, including a fiercely resistant Bunyoro. Uganda was developed as a producer of cotton and later coffee, grown largely by African smallholders rather than on settler estates. By the mid-twentieth century, however, the special status of Buganda and the uneven development between regions had sown tensions that would complicate the path to independence.

events.title: Uganda Protectorate declared
events.summary: Britain proclaims a protectorate over Buganda, soon extended across the wider territory, beginning sixty-eight years of colonial rule administered through the kingdoms.

events.title: The Uganda Railway is built
events.summary: The railway is driven from Mombasa to Kisumu on Lake Victoria by some 32,000 indentured Indian labourers, binding Uganda to the coast and bringing a lasting South Asian presence.

events.title: The Buganda Agreement
events.summary: Sir Harry Johnston and Buganda's regents sign the agreement recognising the kabaka and Lukiiko, creating the 'mailo' land system and entrenching Buganda's privileged status under indirect rule.

events.title: Path to independence negotiated
events.summary: Constitutional conferences in London settle a federal arrangement preserving Buganda's special status, clearing the way for Ugandan self-government and independence.

figures.name: Sir Apolo Kagwa
figures.blurb: The leading Protestant chief and regent for the young kabaka, who signed the Buganda Agreement of 1900 and made Buganda's chiefly establishment central to British indirect rule.

pullquote.text: The Buganda Agreement of 1900 gave the kingdom a privileged, semi-autonomous place within the protectorate that lasted more than fifty years.

eras.title: Independence and the First Obote Era
eras.standfirst: Uganda became independent under Milton Obote with the kabaka as president, but in 1966 Obote crushed Buganda and abolished the kingdoms.
body: Uganda gained independence from the United Kingdom on 9 October 1962. Milton Obote, leader of the Uganda People's Congress (UPC), became prime minister at the head of an alliance with the Buganda royalist party Kabaka Yekka. The independence constitution gave Buganda and the other kingdoms a federal status, and in October 1963 the kabaka of Buganda, Sir Edward Mutesa II, was elected the country's first president, a largely ceremonial office. The new state thus rested on an uneasy bargain between the centralising ambitions of Obote's government and the autonomy of the kingdoms, above all Buganda.

That bargain broke down in 1966. Weakened by the 'lost counties' referendum of 1964, which had returned two counties from Buganda to Bunyoro, and by a gold-smuggling scandal in early 1966, Obote moved against his rivals. In February 1966 he arrested several ministers, suspended the 1962 constitution and assumed full powers. When the Buganda Lukiiko demanded that the central government leave Buganda's soil, Obote ordered the army, led by his commander Idi Amin, to storm the kabaka's palace at Mengo on 24 May 1966. Hundreds were killed, the palace was destroyed, and Mutesa II fled into exile in Britain, where he died in 1969.

Obote then dismantled the old order entirely. A new republican constitution in 1967 abolished the kingdoms of Buganda, Bunyoro, Ankole and Toro, ended their federal privileges and made Obote executive president of a unitary state. He shifted toward a more radical, state-centred politics under his 'Common Man's Charter' of 1969, but his rule grew increasingly dependent on the army. In January 1971, while Obote was abroad, Idi Amin — the general who had stormed the palace five years earlier — seized power in a coup.

events.title: Uganda gains independence
events.summary: Uganda becomes independent from the United Kingdom with Milton Obote as prime minister; the constitution preserves a federal status for Buganda and the other kingdoms.

events.title: Mutesa II becomes first president
events.summary: Uganda becomes a republic and the kabaka of Buganda, Sir Edward Mutesa II, is elected its first president, a largely ceremonial post under the 1962 constitution.

events.title: Battle of Mengo and the fall of Buganda
events.summary: Obote suspends the constitution and orders the army under Idi Amin to storm the kabaka's palace; hundreds die and Mutesa II flees into exile, dying in London in 1969.

events.title: Kingdoms abolished
events.summary: A new republican constitution abolishes the kingdoms of Buganda, Bunyoro, Ankole and Toro and makes Obote executive president of a centralised, unitary Uganda.

events.title: Amin's coup
events.summary: While Obote is abroad, army commander Idi Amin overthrows the government in a military coup, beginning eight years of dictatorship.

figures.name: Milton Obote
figures.blurb: Leader of the Uganda People's Congress and Uganda's first prime minister at independence, who crushed Buganda in 1966 and abolished the kingdoms, before being overthrown by Idi Amin in 1971.

figures.name: Sir Edward Mutesa II
figures.role: Kabaka of Buganda; first president of Uganda
figures.blurb: The thirty-fifth kabaka of Buganda and Uganda's first president, ousted when Obote stormed his palace in 1966; he died in exile in London in 1969.

pullquote.text: In 1966 the army stormed the kabaka's palace at Mengo; within a year the ancient kingdoms of Uganda had been abolished.

eras.title: Idi Amin and the War that Ousted Him
eras.standfirst: Idi Amin's dictatorship brought mass killing and the expulsion of the Asian community, until a war with Tanzania drove him from power.
body: Idi Amin's eight-year rule was among the most violent in modern African history. Initially welcomed by some Baganda for allowing Mutesa II's body to be returned for burial, Amin quickly turned to terror. He purged the army of soldiers from Obote's Acholi and Lango communities, killing thousands, and his regime murdered intellectuals, clergy, judges and ordinary citizens through agencies such as the State Research Bureau. Estimates of those killed during his rule commonly range up to about 300,000. The Anglican archbishop Janani Luwum was among the victims, killed in 1977 after speaking out against the regime.

In August 1972, Amin ordered the expulsion of Uganda's Asian community — some 80,000 people, many of them families settled for generations — giving them about ninety days to leave with almost none of their property. He claimed, citing a dream, that Asians were sabotaging the economy. The expulsion, and Amin's seizure of Asian-owned businesses for redistribution to his cronies, gutted Uganda's commercial sector and accelerated economic collapse, while tens of thousands of those expelled resettled in Britain, India and elsewhere.

Amin's downfall came through military overreach. In late 1978 his forces invaded northern Tanzania and annexed the Kagera salient. Tanzania's president Julius Nyerere ordered a counter-offensive, and the Tanzanian army, joined by Ugandan exiles, drove into Uganda and captured Kampala in April 1979, ending Amin's rule; he fled into exile. There followed a period of instability under short-lived presidents Yusuf Lule and Godfrey Binaisa, and a Military Commission, before disputed elections in December 1980 returned Milton Obote to power for a second time.

events.title: Expulsion of the Asian community
events.summary: Amin orders the roughly 80,000 South Asians in Uganda to leave within about ninety days, seizing their property and businesses and devastating the economy.

events.title: Mass killings under Amin
events.summary: Amin's regime murders soldiers, professionals, clergy and dissidents through bodies such as the State Research Bureau; estimates of the dead run up to about 300,000.

events.title: Murder of Archbishop Luwum
events.summary: The Anglican archbishop Janani Luwum, who had protested against state violence, is killed; his death becomes a symbol of the regime's brutality.

events.title: Uganda–Tanzania War
events.summary: After Amin invades northern Tanzania, Julius Nyerere's forces and Ugandan exiles counter-attack and capture Kampala in April 1979, driving Amin into exile.

events.title: Obote returns to power
events.summary: After interim governments under Lule and Binaisa, disputed elections return Milton Obote and the UPC to power; opponents allege the vote was rigged.

figures.name: Idi Amin
figures.blurb: Army commander who seized power in 1971 and ruled by terror, expelling the Asian community in 1972 and presiding over the killing of up to 300,000 people before a war with Tanzania ousted him.

pullquote.text: Citing a dream, Amin gave some 80,000 Asians ninety days to leave Uganda, gutting the economy almost overnight.

eras.title: The Bush War, Museveni and the Northern Insurgency
eras.standfirst: Yoweri Museveni's guerrilla war brought him to power in 1986 and a long presidency, while the Lord's Resistance Army ravaged the north for two decades.
body: After the disputed 1980 election, Yoweri Museveni, who had served in the governments that followed Amin, alleged fraud and launched an armed rebellion. From 1981 his National Resistance Army (NRA) waged a guerrilla 'Bush War' against Obote's government, based largely in the Luwero Triangle north of Kampala, where the counter-insurgency by government forces killed and displaced many thousands of civilians. Obote was overthrown by his own army in July 1985, but the NRA fought on against the short-lived military government of Tito Okello and captured Kampala on 26 January 1986. Museveni was sworn in as president, ending years of upheaval and promising a 'fundamental change'.

Museveni's victory did not bring peace to the whole country. In the north, among the Acholi who had dominated the former national army, resentment and fear of the new government fed a series of rebellions. Out of these emerged the Lord's Resistance Army (LRA), led from 1987 by the self-styled spirit medium Joseph Kony. Over nearly two decades the LRA waged a horrific insurgency marked by massacres, mutilation and the abduction of tens of thousands of children as fighters and sexual slaves; at its height the conflict displaced nearly two million people into camps before the LRA was pushed out of Uganda after 2006.

In the rest of the country, Museveni won credit for restoring order and reviving the economy, and his government restored the cultural (non-political) kingdoms in 1993, allowing a new kabaka of Buganda to be enthroned. Yet his hold on power lengthened into one of Africa's longest tenures. Term limits were removed from the constitution in 2005 and the presidential age limit in 2017, and Museveni — re-elected repeatedly amid opposition complaints of repression — remained president into the 2020s, more than three decades after first taking Kampala.

events.title: The Ugandan Bush War
events.summary: Museveni's National Resistance Army wages guerrilla war against Obote's government from the Luwero Triangle; the army's counter-insurgency kills and displaces many thousands of civilians.

events.title: Museveni captures Kampala
events.summary: The National Resistance Army takes Kampala and Yoweri Museveni is sworn in as president, ending the post-Amin upheaval and beginning his long rule.

events.title: Rise of the Lord's Resistance Army
events.summary: Joseph Kony forms the Lord's Resistance Army in northern Uganda, launching an insurgency notorious for atrocities and the abduction of children.

events.title: Cultural kingdoms restored
events.summary: Museveni's government restores the kingdoms in a cultural, non-political form; a new kabaka of Buganda, Ronald Mutebi II, is enthroned.

events.title: Term limits removed
events.summary: Parliament removes presidential term limits from the constitution, and Uganda returns to multiparty politics; Museveni's tenure lengthens into one of Africa's longest.

events.title: LRA driven from Uganda
events.summary: After nearly two decades that displaced almost two million people and saw tens of thousands of children abducted, the Lord's Resistance Army is pushed out of northern Uganda.

figures.name: Yoweri Museveni
figures.blurb: Guerrilla leader of the National Resistance Army who seized Kampala in 1986 and went on to rule for more than three decades, restoring stability and the economy but removing constitutional limits on his power.

figures.name: Joseph Kony
figures.role: Leader of the Lord's Resistance Army
figures.blurb: Self-proclaimed spirit medium who led the Lord's Resistance Army from 1987 in a brutal insurgency in northern Uganda, later indicted by the International Criminal Court for crimes against humanity.

pullquote.text: At its height the Lord's Resistance Army drove nearly two million people from their homes and abducted tens of thousands of children.
pullquote.attribution: Human Rights Watch on the LRA

figures.name: Mutesa I
figures.blurb: The powerful nineteenth-century kabaka who opened Buganda to Swahili-Arab traders and the first Christian missionaries, balancing the rival faiths at his court.

figures.name: Milton Obote
figures.role: First prime minister of Uganda (1962–66); president
figures.blurb: Uganda's founding prime minister, who abolished the kingdoms in 1966–67 and twice held power, before and after Idi Amin's dictatorship.

figures.name: Yoweri Museveni
figures.blurb: Bush War guerrilla leader who took power in 1986 and became one of Africa's longest-serving heads of state, presiding over recovery, the defeat of the LRA, and the erosion of term limits.
